title: English Language Learners at Home: Supporting Academic Language Without Losing Your First Language
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Your child is learning English at school. The teachers may have told you to speak English at home to help them catch up. You want your child to succeed academically, and you are willing to do whatever it takes. But the advice to switch to English at home is based on a misunderstanding of how bilingualism works. Your first language is not an obstacle to your child's English development. It is a cognitive asset, and maintaining it benefits your child in ways that extend far beyond language. Here is what the research actually says about supporting English language learners at home.

The Bilingual Advantage Is Real

For decades, educators and parents worried that exposure to two languages would confuse children and delay their language development. That concern has been thoroughly debunked by research. Bilingual children are not confused. They are developing two linguistic systems simultaneously, and the cognitive demands of managing those systems produce benefits that extend beyond language. Bilingual children tend to outperform monolingual children on tasks that require executive functioning: focusing attention, ignoring distractions, switching between tasks, and solving problems that require flexible thinking. The bilingual brain is constantly practicing these skills by selecting the appropriate language for the context and suppressing the other. The practice strengthens the neural circuits that support cognitive control.

These cognitive benefits persist throughout life. Bilingual adults show delayed onset of dementia symptoms compared to monolingual adults, even when other factors are controlled. The bilingual brain builds cognitive reserve, a buffer against age-related decline. The first language you speak to your child is not just preserving a cultural connection. It is building a brain that will serve them for the rest of their life.

Academic research also shows that strong first-language skills support second-language acquisition. A child who has a rich vocabulary and strong narrative skills in their first language can transfer those skills to English. The concept of a story, the understanding that words can be broken into sounds, the knowledge that print carries meaning, all of these are language-independent. They develop in the first language and transfer to the second. The child who enters school with strong first-language literacy has a foundation that supports English literacy.

How to Support Both Languages at Home

Speak the language you speak best. Your child needs to hear rich, complex, grammatically correct language from you. If you are more fluent in your first language, that is the language in which you can provide the richest language input. The English input your child receives at school is sufficient for English acquisition. What they need from you is not more English. It is deep, meaningful conversation in whatever language you speak most comfortably.

Read to your child in both languages. If you are literate in your first language, read books in that language. If you are not, tell stories, sing songs, and engage in oral language traditions in your first language. Reading in English is also valuable, even if your English is not perfect. Your child benefits from the shared experience of reading together, regardless of the language of the book. The library may have bilingual books or books in your first language. Ask what is available.

Do not stop speaking your first language because your child is learning English. The bilingual brain does not have limited capacity. Adding English does not require subtracting the first language. Children who maintain their first language while acquiring English ultimately have better outcomes in both languages than children who lose their first language in the process of acquiring English. The loss of the first language, called subtractive bilingualism, is associated with weaker academic outcomes and weaker family relationships. The maintenance of the first language alongside English, called additive bilingualism, is associated with stronger outcomes in both domains.

Support academic English development through exposure to books, educational media, and conversation about school topics. Ask your child to tell you what they learned in school, in whichever language they prefer. The act of explaining academic content reinforces learning and builds academic vocabulary. If your child prefers to explain in English, listen and ask questions. If they prefer your first language, engage with them in that language. The content knowledge transfers across languages.

What to Do If the School Recommends English-Only at Home

Some teachers and schools still give the outdated advice to switch to English at home. If you receive this advice, respond respectfully but firmly. Explain that you understand the importance of English and are fully supportive of your child's English development at school. Explain that you are also committed to maintaining your first language at home, and that you understand the research shows this supports, rather than hinders, English acquisition. You can offer to provide the teacher with resources on bilingual development if they seem open to learning. You can also direct them to the American Speech-Language-Hearing Association or the National Association for Bilingual Education, both of which support home language maintenance.

If the school is genuinely concerned about your child's English development, ask for specific information. Is your child receiving English language development services? What is their current English proficiency level? What are the benchmarks for progress? A school that is providing appropriate English language support should be able to answer these questions. If your child is not making expected progress, the issue may be the quality or intensity of the English instruction they are receiving, not the language you speak at home.

The Bottom Line

Your first language is not an obstacle to your child's success. It is a cognitive asset and a cultural connection worth preserving. Speak the language you speak best at home. Read and tell stories in both languages. Support academic learning through conversation about school. If the school recommends English-only, respectfully share what the research says and advocate for your child's bilingual development. The goal is not for your child to replace their first language with English. It is for them to build English on the strong foundation your first language provides.
